section.title: Judgment for Idolatry in Egypt
section.sectionRationale: Settled in Egypt against God's word, the remnant does not merely fail to repent, it doubles down on the very sin that destroyed Jerusalem, openly burning incense to the Queen of Heaven and blaming their troubles on having ever stopped. Jeremiah's final recorded confrontation with them ends in a flat impasse: they refuse to listen, and God gives Pharaoh Hophra's own downfall as the sign proving whose word will stand, His or theirs.
section.thesis: A people relocated by their own disobedience still refuse to change, defending their idolatry to Jeremiah's face and blaming disaster on the very repentance that never truly happened, so God gives them one final sign to prove that His word, not their comforting nostalgia, will stand.
section.frameNote: Four scenes: judgment recalled, Jerusalem's ruin traced directly to idolatry; the people's brazen refusal, defending the Queen of Heaven to Jeremiah's face; Jeremiah's reply naming exactly why the disaster came; and a sign given, Pharaoh Hophra's coming downfall, to prove whose word stands.

units.label: Judgment recalled: Jerusalem's ruin from idolatry
units.note: the lesson of the ruins, unlearned

verses.text: This is the word that came to Jeremiah concerning all the Jews living in the land of Egypt, in Migdol, Tahpanhes, and Memphis, and in the land of Pathros:

“This is what the LORD of Hosts, the God of Israel, says: You have seen all the disaster that I brought against Jerusalem and all the cities of Judah; and behold, they lie today in ruins and desolation

because of the evil they have done. 
They provoked Me to anger by continuing to burn incense and to serve other gods that neither they nor you nor your fathers ever knew.
Yet I sent you all My servants the prophets again and again, saying: ‘Do not do this detestable thing that I hate.’
But they did not listen or incline their ears; they did not turn from their wickedness or stop burning incense to other gods.
Therefore My wrath and anger poured out and burned in the cities of Judah and in the streets of Jerusalem, so that they have become the desolate ruin they are today.

So now, this is what the LORD God of Hosts, the God of Israel, says: Why are you doing such great harm to yourselves by cutting off from Judah man and woman, child and infant, leaving yourselves without a remnant?

Why are you provoking Me to anger by the work of your hands by burning incense to other gods in the land of Egypt, where you have gone to reside? 
As a result, you will be cut off and will become an object of cursing and reproach among all the nations of the earth.

Have you forgotten the wickedness of your fathers and of the kings of Judah and their wives, as well as the wickedness that you and your wives committed in the land of Judah and in the streets of Jerusalem?
To this day they have not humbled themselves or shown reverence, nor have they followed My instruction or the statutes that I set before you and your fathers.
Therefore this is what the LORD of Hosts, the God of Israel, says: I will set My face to bring disaster and to cut off all Judah.

And I will take away the remnant of Judah who have resolved to go to the land of Egypt to reside there; they will meet their end. They will all fall by the sword or be consumed by famine. From the least to the greatest, they will die by sword or famine; and they will become an object of cursing and horror, of vilification and reproach.
I will punish those who live in the land of Egypt, just as I punished Jerusalem, by sword and famine and plague,

so that none of the remnant of Judah who have gone to reside in Egypt will escape or survive to return to the land of Judah, where they long to return and live; for none will return except a few fugitives.”

units.label: The people refuse, defend the Queen of Heaven
units.note: blaming disaster on the wrong cause

verses.text: Then all the men who knew that their wives were burning incense to other gods, and all the women standing by, a great assembly, along with all the people living in the land of Egypt and in Pathros, said to Jeremiah,
“As for the word you have spoken to us in the name of the LORD, we will not listen to you!

Instead, we will do everything we vowed to do: We will burn incense to the {{malkath|Queen of Heaven}} and offer drink offerings to her, just as we, our fathers, our kings, and our officials did in the cities of Judah and in the streets of Jerusalem. 
At that time we had plenty of food and good things, and we saw no disaster.
But from the time we stopped burning incense to the Queen of Heaven and pouring out drink offerings to her, we have lacked everything and have been perishing by sword and famine.”

“Moreover,” said the women, “when we burned incense to the Queen of Heaven and poured out drink offerings to her, was it without our husbands' knowledge that we made sacrificial cakes in her image and poured out drink offerings to her?”

units.label: Jeremiah's reply: this is why the disaster came

verses.text: Then Jeremiah said to all the people, both men and women, who were answering him,

“As for the incense you burned in the cities of Judah and in the streets of Jerusalem, you, your fathers, your kings, your officials, and the people of the land, did the LORD not remember and bring this to mind?
So the LORD could no longer endure the evil deeds and detestable acts you committed, and your land became a desolation, a horror, and an object of cursing, without inhabitant, as it is this day.

Because you burned incense and sinned against the LORD, and did not {{shama|obey}} the voice of the LORD or walk in His instruction, His statutes, and His testimonies, this disaster has befallen you, as you see today.”

units.label: A sign given: Pharaoh Hophra's downfall

verses.text: Then Jeremiah said to all the people, including all the women, “Hear the word of the LORD, all those of Judah who are in the land of Egypt.

This is what the LORD of Hosts, the God of Israel, says: As for you and your wives, you have spoken with your mouths and fulfilled with your hands your words: ‘We will surely perform our vows that we have made to burn incense to the Queen of Heaven and to pour out drink offerings to her.’ Go ahead, then, do what you have promised! Keep your vows!

Nevertheless, hear the word of the LORD, all you people of Judah living in Egypt: Behold, I have sworn by My great name, says the LORD, that never again will any man of Judah living in the land of Egypt invoke My name or say, ‘As surely as the Lord GOD lives.’
I am watching over them for harm and not for good, and every man of Judah who is in the land of Egypt will meet his end by sword or famine, until they are finished off.
Those who escape the sword will return from Egypt to Judah, few in number, and the whole remnant of Judah who went to dwell in the land of Egypt will know whose word will stand, Mine or theirs!
This will be a sign to you that I will punish you in this place, declares the LORD, so that you may know that My threats of harm against you will surely stand.

This is what the LORD says: Behold, I will deliver Pharaoh Hophra king of Egypt into the hands of his enemies who seek his life, just as I delivered Zedekiah king of Judah into the hand of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, the enemy who was seeking his life.”

malkath.gloss: The Queen of Heaven, likely a title for the goddess Ishtar or Astarte, worshiped with incense, drink offerings, and sacrificial cakes shaped in her image. The people credit her, not the LORD, for their past prosperity, inverting cause and effect entirely.

shama.gloss: To hear, and by extension to obey, since in Hebrew thought truly hearing a command means doing it. Jeremiah names the people's failure to shama, obey, the voice of the LORD as the actual, plain cause of the disaster they insist on blaming elsewhere.

basic.context: In Egypt, the very place God had warned them not to flee to, the remnant does not repent but doubles down, openly burning incense and pouring drink offerings to the Queen of Heaven, a pagan goddess. When Jeremiah confronts them, they answer with a stunning inversion of cause and effect: they credit their old idolatry with the prosperity they remember and blame their recent troubles on having stopped it, not on the sin itself. Jeremiah restates plainly that their idolatry, not its interruption, destroyed Jerusalem, and God gives a specific, falsifiable sign, the coming downfall of Pharaoh Hophra, to prove once for all whose word will actually stand.
basic.hermeneutics: This is the starkest picture in the book of a hardened conscience rationalizing sin by rewriting its own history, remembering false prosperity under idolatry and blaming disaster on repentance rather than rebellion. Read straightforwardly, the passage does not merely describe stubbornness, it shows a whole community organizing its collective memory around a self-serving lie, and Jeremiah's plain restatement of the facts, verses 21 to 23, is offered as the corrective standard for testing any such comfortable rewriting of history.
basic.originalLanguages: The people insist on continuing worship of the malkath hashamayim, the Queen of Heaven, crediting her rather than the LORD for their prosperity. Jeremiah's answer turns on shama, to hear and obey, the one thing they conspicuously did not do, and their disaster follows directly from that failure, not from any change in ritual practice toward a pagan goddess.
basic.typology: The people's readiness to keep worshiping a false queen of heaven, imagined as the true source of provision, anticipates every later idolatry that credits creation or human power for gifts that belong to the Creator alone.
basic.christ: This chapter shows humanity's native tendency at its bluntest, worshiping the creature rather than the Creator and calling that worship the source of blessing, exactly what Paul describes in Romans 1:25. Christ, the true and better provider of every good gift, exposes and reverses this exchange, calling people not to a queen of heaven but to the living God who alone gives daily bread, and whose Son alone secures the true and lasting rest that idols only ever promised falsely.
basic.god: God's patience has clearly reached its limit with this generation; having sent prophet after prophet and endured Jerusalem's fall itself as an object lesson, He now simply states what will happen and lets a falsifiable sign, Pharaoh Hophra's downfall, prove His word true. Yet even in pronouncing near total judgment, God specifies that a few fugitives will return, verse 14, and that some will survive, verse 28, keeping His promise of an unbroken remnant even here.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 1:25 describes exchanging the truth of God for a lie and worshiping the creature rather than the Creator. Deuteronomy 4:19 forbids worship of the host of heaven, the very category the Queen of Heaven belongs to. Jeremiah 7:18 already described this same worship, families baking cakes for the queen of heaven, back in Judah before the exile. 1 Kings 22:28 records a similar falsifiable prophetic test, if you return in peace, the LORD has not spoken by me.
basic.oneStory: A remnant that fled to Egypt for safety instead doubles down on the idolatry that destroyed their homeland, rewriting their own history to blame repentance rather than rebellion, until God gives a plain, testable sign to settle whose word will stand. This is the whole Bible's diagnosis of the human heart, quick to worship the creature and call it blessing, a diagnosis answered only when Christ turns hearts back to worship the Creator alone.

context.literary: A confrontation narrative structured as accusation, brazen self-justification, plain rebuttal, and a decisive, testable sign, the sharpest and most direct clash between prophet and people recorded anywhere in the book.
context.historical: Set among the Judean refugee communities scattered across Lower Egypt, Migdol, Tahpanhes, Memphis, and Pathros, likely in the years shortly after 586 BC, addressing worship of the Queen of Heaven, a fertility and astral goddess cult attested both in Judah, Jeremiah 7:18, and across the ancient Near East.
context.canonical: The last extended narrative confrontation in the book between Jeremiah and the people, closing the historical section of the book on a note of unrepentant hardness before the oracles against the nations begin in chapter 46.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record the remnant's brazen, self-justifying continuation of the very idolatry that destroyed Jerusalem, and to establish, through the falsifiable sign of Pharaoh Hophra's coming downfall, that God's word rather than the people's comforting revision of history would prove true.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This is historical narrative describing a specific community's specific idolatry and its specific consequence; it does not catalog every possible sin. Its enduring, transferable principle is that misremembering cause and effect, crediting sin with blessing and repentance with disaster, is a persistent and self-destructive pattern of the unrepentant heart in every generation.

debates.question: Why does Jeremiah tell the people to go ahead and keep their vows to the Queen of Heaven, verse 25?

views.label: Bitter irony exposing their hardness
views.case: Jeremiah is not endorsing their idolatry but exposing, with heavy irony, that they have already resolved to disobey regardless of what he says, so his words simply state what is already their fixed intention, letting their own stubbornness stand self-condemned.

views.label: A form of judicial hardening
views.case: Similar to Isaiah 6 or Pharaoh's own hardened heart, God sometimes confirms a people in the very course they have already chosen when they have repeatedly refused correction, letting the consequences of that confirmed choice teach what warning alone could not.

debates.bearing: Either way, the statement functions as a final, sobering pronouncement, not permission, underscoring that a heart which will not repent when clearly warned may eventually be given over to the very sin it insists on keeping.

study.originalLanguages: Continued devotion to the malkath hashamayim, Queen of Heaven, involves incense, drink offerings, and cakes shaped in her image, a full domestic and communal ritual system the people insist restored their prosperity. Jeremiah's rebuttal rests entirely on shama, to hear and obey, the one act consistently absent from their history, proving that the disaster followed their disobedience, not any lapse in devotion to a false goddess.

typology.type: Worship credited to the Queen of Heaven for blessings that belong to the LORD alone, Jeremiah 44:17-18
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates every exchange of the Creator's glory for created things, named directly in Romans 1:25, worshiping and serving the creature rather than the Creator.

study.christ: The remnant's insistence that a false queen of heaven, not the LORD, provided their bread and safety is the exact exchange Paul names in Romans 1:25, worshiping and serving the creature rather than the Creator, who is blessed forever. Christ stands as the full reversal of this exchange, the true bread from heaven, John 6:35, who alone gives what idols only ever falsely promised, calling worshipers of every false queen and false provider back to the one Creator who actually sustains them.
study.god: God's patience with this generation has run its full course, having sent prophet after prophet, Jerusalem's own fall as an unmistakable object lesson, and still met with brazen self-justification; He now simply states the consequence and offers a falsifiable, historically verifiable sign, Pharaoh Hophra's downfall, so that no one could later claim His word had failed to come to pass. Even so, His commitment to preserve a remnant does not vanish entirely, since a few fugitives are promised to survive and return, showing judgment this severe still stops short of total, final annihilation.

crossReferences.note: Describes exchanging the truth of God for a lie, worshiping and serving the creature rather than the Creator, exactly the inversion the remnant commits toward the Queen of Heaven.
Already described this same domestic worship of the queen of heaven back in Judah, showing the sin the exile was meant to break was instead carried intact into Egypt.
Forbids worship of the sun, moon, and host of heaven, the broader category of astral worship the Queen of Heaven cult belonged to.
Micaiah's words, if you return in peace, the LORD has not spoken by me, is the same kind of falsifiable prophetic test God gives here through Pharaoh Hophra's foretold downfall.

study.oneStory: A remnant that fled to Egypt against God's word doubles down there on the very idolatry that destroyed their homeland, rewriting their own history to credit a false goddess with blessing and blame repentance for disaster, until God gives a plain, testable sign to settle the matter for good. This is the Bible's clearest picture of the human heart exchanging the Creator for the creature, a pattern only undone when Christ, the true bread from heaven, turns worship back to the one God who actually gives every good gift.

quiz.q: What does God say destroyed Jerusalem and the cities of Judah?

options.text: Their persistent idolatry, burning incense and serving other gods despite repeated warnings from the prophets.
options.note: Verses 2 to 6 state this cause directly and repeatedly.

options.text: A famine unrelated to any spiritual cause.
options.note: The disaster is explicitly tied to idolatry and disobedience, not an unrelated natural famine.

options.text: Their failure to pay tribute to Babylon on time.
options.note: No mention is made of tribute payments as the cause; the stated cause is persistent idolatry.

options.text: The moral failure of a single king alone.
options.note: The text spreads responsibility broadly, kings, officials, and people alike, not one king in isolation.

quiz.q: How do the people in Egypt respond to Jeremiah's warning against continuing to worship the Queen of Heaven?

options.text: They flatly refuse to listen and insist on continuing their vows to her, crediting her with their past prosperity.
options.note: Verses 16 to 18 record this direct refusal and their claimed reasoning.

options.text: They immediately repent and destroy their idols.
options.note: They do the opposite, openly refusing to stop and insisting on continuing the worship.

options.text: They ask Jeremiah to pray for forgiveness on their behalf.
options.note: They explicitly refuse to listen to Jeremiah's word at all, rather than asking for intercession.

options.text: Only the men continue the practice while the women object.
options.note: Both the men and the women defend the practice together, as verse 19's women's testimony shows.

quiz.q: What reasoning do the people give for continuing to worship the Queen of Heaven?

options.text: They claim they had plenty of food and no disaster while worshiping her, but have lacked everything since stopping.
options.note: Verses 17 to 18 record this exact claimed cause and effect, inverted from the truth Jeremiah states.

options.text: They claim the LORD personally commanded them to worship her.
options.note: They never claim the LORD commanded this worship; they credit the Queen of Heaven herself with their prosperity.

options.text: They claim it is required by Egyptian law.
options.note: No claim of Egyptian legal requirement is made; their reasoning is about remembered prosperity under her worship.

options.text: They claim Jeremiah himself once approved of the practice.
options.note: They never claim Jeremiah's approval; in fact they accuse him of speaking falsely against them.

quiz.q: How does Jeremiah correct their claimed cause and effect?

options.text: He states that it was precisely their idolatry and disobedience to the LORD's voice that caused the disaster, not its interruption.
options.note: Verses 21 to 23 state this correction directly, naming disobedience as the actual cause.

options.text: He agrees that stopping the worship caused their troubles.
options.note: Jeremiah directly contradicts their claim, insisting idolatry itself, not its cessation, caused the disaster.

options.text: He says the disaster had no connection to their religious practices at all.
options.note: Jeremiah explicitly ties the disaster to their religious practice of idolatry, not to unrelated causes.

options.text: He refuses to answer and remains silent.
options.note: Jeremiah gives a direct, detailed answer in verses 20 to 23, not silence.

quiz.q: What sign does God give to prove whose word will stand, His or the people's?

options.text: The coming downfall of Pharaoh Hophra, delivered into the hands of his enemies just as Zedekiah was delivered to Nebuchadnezzar.
options.note: Verse 30 gives this specific, falsifiable sign naming Pharaoh Hophra by name.

options.text: An immediate famine that same week in Egypt.
options.note: The specific sign given is the future downfall of Pharaoh Hophra, not an immediate famine.

options.text: The rebuilding of Jerusalem's temple within the year.
options.note: No promise of imminent temple rebuilding appears in this passage; the sign concerns Pharaoh Hophra's downfall.

options.text: A message written on a scroll and buried at Tahpanhes.
options.note: The scroll and burial sign belongs to the previous chapter's stones symbolizing Nebuchadnezzar's throne, not this chapter's spoken sign about Pharaoh Hophra.

questions: The people credited a false goddess with prosperity that actually came from the LORD, and blamed disaster on the wrong cause entirely. Where might you be crediting the wrong source for blessings that are actually gifts from God?
This remnant rewrote their own history to justify continuing in sin. Where might you be quietly reshaping your memory of a hard season to avoid facing what actually needs to change in you?
The people said plainly, we will not listen to you, refusing correction even from God's own prophet. What correction from God's word are you currently resisting, and why?

God's patience with this generation had run its course after decades of warning and Jerusalem's own fall as an object lesson. How does this sober reality shape how seriously you take God's warnings in your own life right now?
Even in pronouncing severe judgment, God still promised a few fugitives would survive and return. Where do you need to trust that God's mercy remains real even in the middle of deserved consequences?

translationNotes.note: Migdol, Tahpanhes, Memphis, and Pathros name the main regions of Judean settlement in Egypt, from the northeastern delta down to Upper Egypt, showing the refugee community had already spread well beyond Tahpanhes alone by this point.

The Queen of Heaven is widely identified with Ishtar or Astarte, a Mesopotamian and Canaanite fertility and astral goddess whose worship, attested also in Jeremiah 7:18, apparently persisted as a household practice even among otherwise observant Judean families.

Pharaoh Hophra, known in Egyptian records as Apries, did in fact later face a military coup and was eventually killed by his own general Amasis, an outcome later writers connect to the fulfillment of this exact prophecy.
